serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Baingan Bharta is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as served in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (2017)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Baingan Bharta are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition), using the cooked brinjal dish entry as the primary anchor. Energy (112 kcal/100g), protein (2.4g), fat (7.2g), carbohydrate (10.0g), dietary fibre (3.2g), sodium (260mg), calcium (24mg), iron (0.8mg), and vitamin C (10mg) are consistent with ICMR-NIN data for a home-style or restaurant-style baingan bharta with added oil and spices. Cholesterol (0.0mg) and trans fat (0.0g) are confirmed zero as the dish is fully plant-based with no animal fat. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is consistent with use of a small quantity of vegetable oil. The original input record listed 'grabfood' as data_source with confidence 0.7 (ai_generated provenance); all values have been cross-referenced against ICMR-NIN and are retained as plausible adapted estimates. ICMR-NIN is the approved primary source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Baingan Bharta exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the GI of cooked eggplant/aubergine (GI ~15, low GI vegetable) as reported in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, and cross-referenced with the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com). The dish is predominantly eggplant with low-GI vegetables (onion, tomato, garlic) and no high-GI starch components. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 25.0) / 100 = 3.75, rounded to 3.8. Serving carbohydrate = 10.0g/100g × 250g = 25.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 260 mg/100g is below the 400 mg/100g threshold; dish provides potassium from eggplant, tomato, and onion supporting DASH dietary pattern.
hypertension.guidance: Baingan Bharta is suitable for hypertensive patients at standard portions; request reduced added salt during preparation to further lower sodium intake, and pair with low-sodium accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load per serving (3.8); dietary fibre at 3.2g/100g moderates postprandial glucose absorption; total carbohydrate is 10g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baingan Bharta is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI, high fibre content, and low carbohydrate density; avoid pairing with large portions of white rice or naan to prevent overall glycaemic load from rising.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.2g/100g, cholesterol is 0mg/100g, trans fat is 0g/100g; dietary fibre at 3.2g/100g supports LDL reduction; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provide additional cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baingan Bharta is a heart-healthy choice for cardiovascular disease patients; ensure oil quantity is controlled during preparation to limit total fat intake, and prefer grilling or roasting over frying methods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric curcumin, ginger gingerols, garlic) are beneficial; however, eggplant, tomato, and green chili are nightshade vegetables which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (solanine) should monitor symptoms when consuming Baingan Bharta and reduce or eliminate if joint flares occur.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baingan Bharta (also spelled Baingan Bartha or Baigan Bharta) is a classic North Indian vegetarian dish made by roasting or grilling whole brinjal (eggplant/aubergine) over an open flame or in an oven until the skin is charred and the flesh becomes smoky, tender, and soft. The roasted eggplant is then peeled, mashed, and sautéed in oil with a flavourful base of finely chopped onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and fresh green chilies, seasoned with cumin, turmeric, coriander powder, garam masala, and salt. The result is a smoky, earthy, mildly spiced mash with a rich umami depth from the charred eggplant and a tangy brightness from the tomatoes. Baingan Bharta is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal. It is a staple of Punjabi cuisine and is widely served across North India and in Indian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on menus of authentic North Indian restaurants. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 112 kcal, 2.4g protein, 7.2g fat (1.2g saturated), 10g carbohydrate, 3.2g dietary fibre, and 260mg sodium. The dish has a low glycemic index (~15) and a low glycemic load per standard serving, making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. Its high fibre content, low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, garlic) make it a heart-healthy and diabetes-friendly option. It is typically served as a side dish with roti, naan, or rice.

Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry for Baingan Bharta was found in a single approved primary source; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN ingredient-level and dish-level data.

GI is an analogous estimate (~15) based on cooked eggplant GI from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care; no direct GI measurement for Baingan Bharta exists in the approved GI database. 6. This dish is served in Singapore at Indian restaurants and is of North Indian origin; multilingual names provided in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish multilingual requirements. 9.
